channel.title: The Provenance Trail on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner
channel.description: Recent content in The Provenance Trail on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner

item.title: Chapter 10: Advocate, Home and Abroad
item.description:
Chapter 10

Advocate, Home and Abroad

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

Advocate: Home and Abroad

Bob’s Journal entry …

Graham tunneled through security at the airport and made his way down the long corridor to the waiting jet that was ready to thrust him to another continent. Once off the ground, within moments, he would appear like a speck on the horizon. Bob’s sentimental thoughts were brought to an abrupt halt when he realized Graham’s laptop was still at the security-check—forgotten and alone, except for his passport and cash which was tucked in the carrying case.

item.title: Chapter 11: Orrock Esq.
item.description:
Chapter 11

Orrock Esq.

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

730 AS, the Property of James Orrock Esq.

“Hello, this is Jeremy Rex-Parkes, I am away from my desk at the moment, but if you would like to leave your name and number I will get back to you as soon as possible.” Just bad timing! Each time Bob phoned the archivist at Christie’s in he was away. Because of the eight-hour time difference he was calling at then again at in the morning. He left his name and number once, but to no avail. Bob mumbled, “The repetitive nature of the job must have driven poor Jeremy to distraction.” Out of frustration Bob was about to give up. “One last try,” he grumbled.

item.title: Chapter 12: The Jolly Art Sleuth
item.description:
Chapter 12

The Jolly Art Sleuth

Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters

The Jolly Art Sleuth

Shortly after Bob contacted the previously mentioned scientist, Marilyn Laver, and the gathering of material from her 1982 CCI report on yours truly had begun, she sent him a small news clipping. It didn’t seem relevant. It had something to do with a Canadian fellow discovering JMW’s fingerprints on an oil painting. He had bought it from a picker who found it mixed together with a jumble of old and broken frames. The artist was unknown. Surprisingly, the soiled landscape which was later purchased by the Biró family produced the proverbial rags to riches story!
